rationales.title: Substrate as universal representation: plans, prompts, skills, and agents are projections of one atom graph
rationales.narrative:
Thesis: there is one typed graph of atoms (block items) and edges, and a plan, a prompt, a skill, and an agent are not different kinds of thing — they are different projections of that graph. The general form is artifact = render(select(atoms, edges), macro, target): a plan doc selects a plan's atoms + descendants with canonical macros into presentational text; a prompt selects grounding + instruction atoms with prompt-assembly macros into instructional, budgeted text; a skill selects capability + instruction atoms; an agent selects prompt + I/O-schema + tool-grant atoms, rendering the prompt and reading the contract structurally. Two layers unify completely: data (every artifact's content is typed atoms + edges) and text (every prose-for-a-reader artifact is the same composition engine over a different selection). Evidence it is already operating: the prompt corpus is atomized (preamble, voice constraints, per-spec snippets, grounding sections), and the pi SKILL.md files are themselves render(tool-source, skill-macro) — 'Generated from source'.

Where it reaches its limit is NOT text-vs-code (a script's source is content, hence an atom, hence rendered). The real boundary is static content vs dynamic event: everything that can be a file — prose, code, scripts, docs, schemas, configs, binary resources — is an atom; render is a build that materializes the files; the runtime is an evaluator that acts on them. The objects/ content-addressed store is exactly this storage. The execution event is then bracketed by atoms on both sides: the dispatch ('run script.py' + context) is render(atoms), and the result returns as an atom (e.g. run-real-checks → a verification item). The only irreducibles that remain are: (1) the evaluator/runtime — a bootstrapping floor, because at the instant it interprets the substrate it is not a projection of what it interprets; its source can be atoms and it can be built from atoms, but a prior already-running host (pi-dispatch + the model + the OS) runs it; the system is self-describing and self-building but not self-running-from-nothing; (2) the execution act itself — a description of computing is not computing ('2+2' does not add), though it is fenced by input/output atoms; (3) external side effects — effects on systems outside the substrate (a closed generator has none); (4) quality — schema validates well-formedness, never goodness; judgment never fully dissolves into the substrate.

For a jit-agent the distinction between simulating a run (the forbidden LLM self-report, which run-real-checks exists to prevent) and dispatching a real interpreter (the sound path, whose deterministic result is an atom) sits exactly on this boundary. Converged picture: the substrate is the complete program plus its own execution history; pi is the already-running eval over it. For a closed plan generator — composed atoms (catalogues + situation) → generation → render canonical doc, no external system of record — the ONLY non-substrate moments are the model generating and the human reading. To the question 'to what extent is it all substrate': completely at the level of representation, and in proportion to how well the atoms are factored to be shared across projections; the irreducible remainder is the evaluator, the transient compute act, upstream-dependency substance (pinned, not authored), and quality-judgment.
